_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AAOS shoulder guidelines, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) consensus, NICE NG101, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) is an inflammatory condition of the glenohumeral joint capsule characterized by progressive fibrosis and shrinkage, leading to pain, stiffness, and marked restriction of both active and passive shoulder range of motion — meaning the arm cannot be lifted by the patient nor by an examiner. The condition follows a characteristic triphasic natural history: a painful freezing phase lasting 2-9 months, a stiff frozen phase lasting 4-12 months, and a gradual thawing phase that can extend 5-26 months or longer. Affecting 2-5% of the general population, frozen shoulder predominantly strikes adults aged 40-70, with peak incidence in the mid-50s, and is 3-4 times more common in people with diabetes mellitus. The hallmark distinguishing feature is the loss of passive external rotation — when the arm is held at the side, the examiner cannot rotate it outward — which is present in frozen shoulder but not in rotator cuff pathology. Treatment ranges from conservative management (physical therapy, intra-articular corticosteroid injection) in the majority of cases, to manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release for refractory cases that do not respond to 6-12 months of structured rehabilitation.

keyStats.prevalence: 2-5% of the general population; up to 20-30% of people with diabetes develop frozen shoulder during their lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence ages 40-70, mean age 52; women slightly more affected than men in the general population; no gender difference in diabetic populations
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of onset is 52 years; rare under age 35 and over age 75
keyStats.globalCases: Annual incidence of 0.75-2% in the general population; one of the most common causes of shoulder pain and disability in middle-aged adults

definition: Frozen shoulder (ICD-10: M75.0 for adhesive capsulitis of shoulder; M75.01 right, M75.02 left, M75.03 bilateral) is an inflammatory, fibrosing condition of the glenohumeral joint capsule characterized by synovial inflammation, capsular thickening and contraction, and loss of the normal capsular redundancies. The capsule normally allows approximately 150 degrees of flexion, 90 degrees of abduction, 60 degrees of external rotation, and 70 degrees of internal rotation — in frozen shoulder, all of these ranges are significantly restricted. The pathological hallmark is deposition of type I and type III collagen in the subsynovial layer, accompanied by myofibroblast contraction that actively shrinks the capsule. This is not simply joint stiffness — it is an active inflammatory and fibrotic process. The condition exists on a spectrum with diabetic frozen shoulder being more severe, more likely bilateral, and more resistant to treatment. Frozen shoulder is fundamentally different from rotator cuff pathology — in rotator cuff tears, passive range of motion is preserved while active motion is limited; in frozen shoulder, both active AND passive range of motion are restricted because the capsule itself is shortened.

typesClassification.description: The classic form, developing insidiously without a clear precipitating cause in an otherwise healthy individual. The natural history proceeds through the characteristic three phases: freezing, frozen, and thawing. Most common in non-diabetic patients aged 40-60. Approximately 40% of cases are associated with a period of prior shoulder injury or immobilization that preceded the onset.

Frozen shoulder developing in association with a known predisposing condition, the most significant of which is diabetes mellitus (5-fold increased risk). Other associated conditions include: thyroid disease (hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism), Parkinson's disease, cardiovascular disease, stroke, shoulder surgery or trauma, and prolonged immobilization. Secondary frozen shoulder tends to be more severe, more prolonged, and more resistant to treatment.

A distinct and more severe form occurring in patients with diabetes mellitus. Diabetic patients have a 5-fold increased risk of frozen shoulder, with 10-20% developing the condition. In diabetics, frozen shoulder is more likely to be bilateral (30-40% bilateral vs. 3-5% in non-diabetics), more severe in terms of range of motion loss, and more resistant to treatment. The pathophysiology involves glycosylation of collagen in the joint capsule, making it less extensible and more prone to fibrosis.

Involvement of both shoulders simultaneously (0-15% of cases) or sequentially (15-30% of cases, with the second shoulder typically developing within 5 years of the first). Bilateral involvement always warrants evaluation for systemic causes — diabetes, thyroid disorders, and other metabolic conditions. The second shoulder may develop while the first is still active or after the first has resolved.

primarySymptoms: Progressive loss of shoulder range of motion over weeks to months — the defining symptom. The patient notices increasing difficulty reaching overhead, to the side, behind the back, and rotating the arm outward. Both active and passive motion are restricted.

Pain, particularly at the end range of motion in any direction — typically a deep, aching pain in the shoulder and upper arm, often worse at night. Pain is most severe in the initial freezing phase and may improve as the shoulder enters the frozen stiff phase.

Loss of passive external rotation — the single most discriminating clinical finding. When the arm is held at the side with the elbow at 90 degrees, external rotation is markedly limited (normal: 70-90 degrees; frozen shoulder: 0-20 degrees). This finding distinguishes frozen shoulder from rotator cuff pathology.

Loss of active range of motion in all planes — abduction, forward flexion, internal rotation (reaching behind the back), and external rotation are all restricted. The patient cannot compensate by using other muscles because the joint capsule itself is stiff.
Night pain disrupting sleep, particularly when lying on the affected shoulder — pain often wakes patients from sleep and makes finding a comfortable sleeping position difficult.
Gradual worsening of stiffness over months without significant trauma — the insidious onset is a key diagnostic clue distinguishing frozen shoulder from a rotator cuff tear or fracture.
Loss of internal rotation limiting activities of daily living — difficulty with toileting, dressing (reaching for sleeves), and personal hygiene are often the most disabling functional limitations.
earlyWarningSigns: Shoulder stiffness that is new and progressive — a gradual tightening of the shoulder without a clear injury is the earliest sign
Night pain when lying on the shoulder that was not present before
Difficulty reaching behind the back (e.g., fastening a bra, reaching into a back pocket) — the loss of internal rotation is an early functional clue
Shoulder pain and stiffness developing after a period of shoulder immobilization (e.g., after wearing a sling for an injury)
Shoulder pain and stiffness in a person with diabetes or thyroid disease — these conditions significantly increase frozen shoulder risk
Shoulder pain and stiffness developing in a person over age 45 with no clear injury — peak onset is in the mid-50s
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of severe shoulder pain following trauma — suggests fracture (proximal humerus, clavicle) or dislocation, not frozen shoulder
Shoulder pain, swelling, and warmth with fever — suggests septic arthritis or inflammatory arthritis (rheumatoid arthritis flare), which are surgical/urgent rheumatology emergencies
Acute complete loss of the ability to raise the arm following an injury, with numbness or tingling in the arm — may indicate nerve injury
Sudden severe shoulder pain and inability to move the arm in a person with known cancer — possible pathologic fracture
Shoulder pain with associated chest pain, shortness of breath, and left arm symptoms — requires immediate cardiac evaluation to rule out cardiac causes of referred shoulder pain

causes.description: The majority of cases have no identifiable cause — the condition simply develops. This is likely a dysregulated wound healing response in which fibroblasts in the subsynovial layer of the capsule are activated by an unknown trigger and deposit excess collagen, while myofibroblasts within the capsule actively contract it. There is evidence of underlying genetic predisposition in some families.

Diabetes mellitus is the single strongest risk factor for frozen shoulder — a 5-fold increased risk compared to the non-diabetic population. The mechanism involves glycosylation of collagen fibers in the joint capsule (advanced glycation end products), which makes the capsule stiffer, less extensible, and more prone to fibrotic changes. Glycemic control (HbA1c level) correlates with severity — patients with poorly controlled diabetes have more severe and more prolonged frozen shoulder.

Both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism are associated with increased frozen shoulder risk — 2-3 fold increased risk. The mechanism is thought to involve autoimmune inflammation and alterations in connective tissue metabolism. Thyroid function testing should be considered in any patient with bilateral frozen shoulder or recurrent frozen shoulder.

Prolonged immobilization of the shoulder — following an injury, after surgery, or during illness — is a well-established trigger for frozen shoulder. Immobilization leads to capsule contraction even without intrinsic capsular pathology. This is why early mobilization after shoulder injury or surgery is critical for prevention.

Any event that causes shoulder pain and leads the patient to limit use of the arm can trigger the inflammatory cascade that progresses to frozen shoulder. This includes rotator cuff injuries, AC joint sprains, biceps tendinitis, and shoulder surgery itself.

Rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, and other connective tissue diseases are associated with increased frozen shoulder risk through inflammatory synovitis affecting the glenohumeral joint. In these patients, frozen shoulder is part of a broader systemic inflammatory picture.

Parkinson's disease, stroke, and cervical radiculopathy are associated with frozen shoulder. In Parkinson's, increased collagen cross-linking and reduced movement contribute. In stroke, hemiplegic shoulder and adhesive capsulitis are common complications of upper motor neuron lesions.

riskFactors.description: Peak incidence in the mid-50s; rare under age 35; the condition is distinctly uncommon in children and adolescents.
The single most powerful risk factor — 5-fold increased risk, 10-20% lifetime prevalence in diabetics. HbA1c level correlates with severity.
2-3 fold increased risk in patients with thyroid disorders. Autoimmune thyroid disease may share immunological pathways with capsular fibrosis.
Slightly more common in women than men in non-diabetic populations (F:M ratio approximately 1.5:1). No gender difference in diabetic populations.
Early, gentle range of motion exercises during any period of shoulder pain or after injury reduces the risk of capsular contracture.
Patients with myocardial infarction, stroke, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease have moderately increased risk of frozen shoulder, potentially mediated through shared inflammatory pathways.
Parkinson's disease increases risk through altered connective tissue metabolism and reduced spontaneous movement.
There is some evidence of familial aggregation — having a first-degree relative with frozen shoulder is associated with a modest increase in risk.

affectedDemographics: Frozen shoulder affects 2-5% of the general population annually; one of the most common causes of shoulder pain and disability in adults aged 40-70
The condition is distinctly rare in people under 35 and over 75 — age distribution is remarkably concentrated in the 40-70 age group
Women are affected slightly more frequently than men in non-diabetic populations (approximately 60% female)
People with diabetes mellitus have a 5-fold increased risk — 10-20% of diabetics will develop frozen shoulder during their lifetime
Bilateral involvement occurs in 3-5% of non-diabetic cases but 30-40% of diabetic cases
The condition is equally common across race/ethnicity groups; prevalence data is consistent across Western countries
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of frozen shoulder is primarily clinical, based on a characteristic history of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness over months with a restricted range of motion affecting both active and passive movement. The key physical examination finding is restriction of passive external rotation — when the arm is held at the side and elbow flexed at 90 degrees, the examiner cannot rotate the arm outward (normal external rotation: 70-90 degrees; frozen shoulder: 0-20 degrees). Active range of motion in all planes is similarly restricted. The examiner must rule out a rotator cuff tear — in which passive motion is preserved. Plain radiographs (X-rays) of the shoulder are obtained to exclude arthritis,骨折, or calcific tendinitis — all of which can cause secondary frozen shoulder. MRI is not routinely required but may be ordered when the diagnosis is uncertain or when surgical intervention is planned, to rule out rotator cuff pathology and assess capsular thickening. In the frozen phase, X-rays are typically normal; in late or chronic cases, osteopenia of the proximal humerus may be seen.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for diagnosis. Characteristic presentation: gradual onset of pain and progressive loss of passive external rotation, pain at the end range of motion in all planes, night pain. The key finding is restricted passive external rotation with the arm at the side — present in >95% of cases. This distinguishes frozen shoulder from rotator cuff tears (passive rotation intact) and impingement (pain with resisted tests).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Your doctor will move your arm in all directions — actively (you move it) and passively (they move it). They will specifically test external rotation with your arm at your side. They will palpate the shoulder and test rotator cuff strength to exclude rotator cuff tears.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiographs (X-rays) — AP, scapular Y, axillary lateral
diagnosticTests.purpose: Ordered to rule out alternative causes of shoulder pain and stiffness: glenohumeral arthritis, AC joint arthritis, calcific tendinitis, proximal humerus or clavicular fractures, and dislocation. Typically normal in primary frozen shoulder. May show osteopenia or subtle arthritic changes in secondary frozen shoulder.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray — three views of the shoulder. Takes 5 minutes, minimal radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routinely needed but useful when the diagnosis is uncertain (to rule out rotator cuff tear, labral tear, or osteonecrosis) or when arthroscopic surgery is planned. In frozen shoulder, MRI typically shows thickening and contrast enhancement of the axillary recess (the most affected part of the capsule), synovial inflammation, and sometimes fluid in the subacromial bursa.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie inside the MRI scanner with your arm positioned. Takes 30-45 minutes. No radiation. You must remain still. The machine is loud; earplugs are provided.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (fasting glucose, HbA1c, TSH, free T4)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Ordered when a systemic cause is suspected — particularly in bilateral frozen shoulder, recurrent frozen shoulder, or frozen shoulder in a patient with symptoms of thyroid disease or diabetes. Screening for diabetes (fasting glucose, HbA1c), thyroid dysfunction (TSH, free T4), and inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) in appropriate cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results typically available within 1-2 days for most tests.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Can demonstrate capsular thickening and synovial inflammation in frozen shoulder, and is useful for ruling out rotator cuff pathology. Less commonly used than MRI but provides useful dynamic information at lower cost.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld ultrasound probe is moved over the shoulder with acoustic gel. Non-invasive, no radiation. Takes 15-20 minutes. Results are immediate.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of frozen shoulder is stage-specific and aims to manage pain during the freezing phase while preventing further capsule contracture, then aggressively restore range of motion during the frozen and thawing phases. The cornerstone of treatment is physical therapy — specifically structured stretching and range of motion exercises performed consistently over many months. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (into the glenohumeral joint, not the subacromial bursa) is the most effective adjunct to physical therapy — corticosteroids reduce synovial inflammation and pain, allowing more effective participation in stretching. NSAIDs are used for pain control. For patients who do not respond to 6-12 months of conservative treatment, procedural interventions include: manipulation under anesthesia (MUA) — forceful ranging of the shoulder while the patient is under anesthesia, which ruptures the contracted capsule — and arthroscopic capsular release — minimally invasive surgery cutting the contracted capsule to restore motion. Both procedures are followed by intensive physical therapy to maintain the gained range of motion. The overall prognosis is favorable — approximately 90% of patients improve with conservative treatment alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy — structured stretching and range of motion program
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of treatment for all phases. Physical therapy includes: passive stretching of the capsule in all restricted directions (external rotation, abduction, internal rotation); active-assisted range of motion exercises; pendulum exercises; and in the thawing phase, progressive strengthening. Treatment is prolonged — typically 6-12 months of consistent therapy. Frequency is typically 2-3 sessions per week with daily home exercises.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The primary evidence-based treatment. Success rates of 70-90% when performed consistently over the full natural course of the condition. Outcomes are directly correlated with patient compliance — those who perform home exercises daily have significantly better outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (glenohumeral joint)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of triamcinolone acetonide or methylprednisolone directly into the glenohumeral joint (not subacromial bursa — injection into the wrong location is a common error) reduces synovial inflammation, reduces pain, and may modestly improve range of motion. Most effective in the early freezing phase when pain is the primary limiting factor. Typically combined with physical therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for pain relief in 70-80% of patients during the freezing phase. Range of motion improvement is more modest. The combination of corticosteroid injection plus physical therapy is more effective than either alone.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs — oral (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) and topical
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications for pain control, particularly night pain. Topical diclofenac gel applied to the anterior shoulder is effective for localized pain with minimal systemic side effects. Oral NSAIDs are most useful in the painful freezing phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for pain control in the short term. They do not alter the underlying capsular fibrosis but make physical therapy more tolerable.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (short course)
medicalTreatments.description: A short tapering course of oral prednisone (e.g., 20 mg daily for 2 weeks, then taper) can reduce inflammation and pain in the early freezing phase, particularly when night pain is severe. Not a long-term solution — the course is brief to minimize steroid side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest short-term pain relief in early-stage frozen shoulder. Not supported as standard of care but used in selected cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrodilatation (distension arthrography)
medicalTreatments.description: A procedure in which contrast dye and corticosteroid are injected into the glenohumeral joint under fluoroscopy (X-ray guidance), with increasing pressure to distend and potentially rupture the contracted capsule. Some evidence supports its use in the frozen phase to accelerate recovery of motion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mixed evidence from RCTs. Some studies show modest benefit in the frozen phase; others show no significant advantage over physical therapy alone.

surgicalOptions.name: Manipulation under anesthesia (MUA)
surgicalOptions.description: A procedure in which the orthopedic surgeon forcefully moves the shoulder through all ranges of motion while the patient is under general anesthesia. The controlled, high-velocity stretch ruptures the contracted anterior and inferior capsule, restoring range of motion. The procedure takes 5-10 minutes. A post-procedure hemarthrosis (bleeding into the joint) is expected and normal.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% good-to-excellent outcomes in properly selected patients with mature, significantly contracted capsules. Most effective when performed after the capsule has fully matured (typically after 6-9 months of conservative treatment). Risks include: proximal humerus fracture (rare), rotator cuff tear (rare), brachial plexus injury (rare), and hemarthrosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic capsular release
surgicalOptions.description: A minimally invasive (arthroscopic) surgical procedure in which the surgeon cuts (releases) the contracted portions of the glenohumeral joint capsule through small portal incisions. The most commonly released structure is the inferior glenohumeral ligament (IGHL) and the axillary recess. Performed as day surgery. Followed by an immediate post-operative physical therapy program — this is not a recovery-in-bed procedure; patients begin stretching within 24-48 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 90-95% good-to-excellent outcomes in patients with refractory frozen shoulder who have failed 6-12 months of conservative treatment. Slightly superior to MUA in terms of precision and restoration of motion. Requires intensive post-operative physical therapy — without it, the capsule re-adheres and stiffness recurs.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined MUA and arthroscopic release (selective cases)
surgicalOptions.description: For severe, refractory cases, some surgeons perform both — first an arthroscopic release of the most contracted portions of the capsule, followed by gentle manipulation to complete the ranging. This combination approach may offer advantages in the most severe cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable to or slightly better than either procedure alone in severely refractory cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Heat therapy before stretching
alternativeTreatments.description: Applying moist heat (warm compress, warm towel, or hot pack) to the shoulder for 15-20 minutes before stretching sessions warms the capsular tissue, making it more extensible and allowing deeper stretching. Simple, low-cost, evidence-supported adjunct.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate benefit — heat increases the extensibility of collagen, allowing for more effective stretching.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Medical acupuncture targeting shoulder musculature and glenohumeral joint region. May provide modest pain relief in the freezing phase. Used as an adjunct to physical therapy, not as a primary treatment.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Low-quality evidence for modest short-term pain relief during the freezing phase.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supervised versus home exercise program
alternativeTreatments.description: Studies consistently show that supervised physical therapy combined with a rigorous home exercise program produces superior outcomes to home exercise alone. The key exercises are: passive external rotation stretch (using a door frame), cross-body adduction stretch, overhead reaching stretch, and pendulum exercises.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Best outcomes achieved with combined supervised physical therapy and consistent daily home exercises.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopedic surgeon specializing in shoulder surgery or a sports medicine physician should be seen when: frozen shoulder has not improved after 6-12 months of structured physical therapy; when pain is severe and not controlled with NSAIDs and corticosteroid injection; when the condition is significantly impairing work or sleep; when bilateral involvement suggests an underlying systemic condition (diabetes, thyroid disease) requiring medical evaluation; or when considering manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an orthopedic surgeon with shoulder surgery fellowship training or a sports medicine physician with a focus on shoulder conditions. Physical therapy with a therapist experienced in shoulder rehabilitation is the most important ongoing clinician relationship — the vast majority of patients are managed by their physical therapist and primary care physician without surgery. Ask the physical therapist about their experience with capsular stretching and range of motion restoration techniques specific to frozen shoulder.
preventionStrategies: Early range of motion after any shoulder injury or surgery — begin gentle passive motion within days of any shoulder injury (with physician guidance) to prevent capsule contracture
Maintaining shoulder mobility through regular stretching — particularly important for people with diabetes, thyroid disease, or a history of frozen shoulder in the opposite shoulder
Optimal glycemic control in diabetic patients — poor glycemic control (elevated HbA1c) is associated with more severe and more prolonged frozen shoulder
Prompt treatment of shoulder pain before it leads to avoidance of use — seeking care early when shoulder pain and stiffness first develop
Regular shoulder exercise in high-risk populations (diabetics, thyroid patients over 40) — daily stretching routines may reduce incidence
lifestyleModifications: Sleep with the arm supported on a pillow — sleeping in a recliner is often most comfortable during the acute painful phase
Use the affected arm within pain limits — avoiding the arm entirely leads to further capsule contracture and deconditioning
Adapt your wardrobe — front-buttoning shirts, pullover tops, and front-close bras reduce the need for difficult reaching motions during the frozen phase
Avoid heavy lifting and overhead reaching with the affected arm during the freezing phase
Apply heat before stretching exercises — 15-20 minutes of moist heat before therapy sessions improves stretching effectiveness
Plan for a prolonged recovery — frozen shoulder takes 1-3 years to fully resolve in many patients; patience is essential

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods — Mediterranean-style diet rich in omega-3 fatty fish, olive oil, vegetables, and nuts reduces systemic inflammation that may amplify the capsular inflammatory process
Optimal glycemic control (for diabetics) — keeping HbA1c within target range is associated with less severe and shorter-duration frozen shoulder
Collagen-supporting nutrients — adequate vitamin C (citrus, peppers, leafy greens), protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight), and zinc support connective tissue remodeling
Water — well-hydrated connective tissue is more extensible than dehydrated tissue
Anti-inflammatory herbs — turmeric (curcumin) and ginger have modest anti-inflammatory effects that may be complementary
avoid: Processed foods and refined carbohydrates — these promote systemic inflammation
Excessive alcohol — alcohol impairs connective tissue remodeling and healing
High-sodium diet — high sodium promotes fluid retention that can worsen shoulder inflammation
Smoking — smoking impairs tissue healing and may contribute to capsular fibrosis

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise therapy for frozen shoulder is the cornerstone of treatment and must be performed consistently for months to years. The key exercises are: (1) Passive external rotation stretch — stand in a doorway with the elbow bent at 90 degrees and forearm pressed against the door frame; rotate the body away from the arm to stretch the external rotators. Hold 30 seconds, repeat 5 times, twice daily. (2) Cross-body adduction stretch — use the opposite arm to pull the affected arm across the chest at shoulder height; hold 30 seconds, repeat 5 times, twice daily. (3) Forward flexion stretch — use the opposite arm to lift the affected arm overhead in a forward arc as high as possible; hold 30 seconds, repeat 5 times, twice daily. (4) Pendulum exercises — lean forward with the good arm supported on a table; let the affected arm hang; gently swing the arm in small circles, gradually increasing diameter. (5) Internal rotation stretch — reach behind the back with the affected arm and use the opposite arm to pull it higher up the back. All stretches should be taken to the end range of motion and held for 30-60 seconds. Heat before stretching improves effectiveness.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed stretches daily — twice daily is the standard recommendation; skipping days significantly slows progress
Use heat (warm compress, warm shower) for 15-20 minutes before stretching sessions to improve tissue extensibility
Apply ice after stretching sessions if the shoulder is painful — 15-20 minutes
Take NSAIDs or acetaminophen before stretching if pain limits participation — pain control enables more effective stretching
Track range of motion monthly — a simple measure like 'how far can you reach behind your back' or 'how high can you reach overhead' provides objective evidence of progress
Accept that progress is measured in months, not weeks — the natural history of frozen shoulder runs over 1-3 years; treatment accelerates the timeline but does not eliminate it
prognosis: Frozen shoulder has a generally favorable prognosis — approximately 90% of patients improve with conservative treatment. However, the condition is characteristically prolonged, typically lasting 1-3 years from onset to full recovery even with optimal treatment. Factors associated with worse prognosis: diabetes mellitus (more severe, more prolonged), severe initial restriction of motion, prolonged symptoms before treatment initiation, bilateral involvement, and poor compliance with physical therapy. Factors associated with better prognosis: rapid onset with early treatment, good initial response to corticosteroids and physical therapy, and absence of metabolic comorbidities. Approximately 5-10% of patients have persistent, functionally limiting stiffness after 2-3 years and are candidates for manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release.
recoveryTimeline: The natural history of frozen shoulder — which treatment accelerates but does not eliminate — proceeds in three phases: Freezing phase (months 0-9): pain increases, range of motion decreases. Physical therapy and corticosteroid injection are most effective here. Frozen phase (months 3-15): pain often decreases but stiffness is maximal. Aggressive stretching is the key intervention. Thawing phase (months 9-42+): range of motion gradually improves. Continuation of stretching is critical — patients who stop exercising during thawing often lose gains. With treatment (physical therapy + corticosteroid injection): the freezing phase may be shortened, pain better controlled, and thawing accelerated. After manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release: dramatic immediate improvement in range of motion, with 6-12 weeks of intensive post-procedure physical therapy to maintain the gains.
complications: Persistent, functionally limiting stiffness — 5-10% of patients have residual stiffness and limited range of motion after 2-3 years, even with treatment
Recurrence — approximately 5-10% of patients experience recurrence in the same shoulder, and 15-30% develop frozen shoulder in the contralateral shoulder (the second shoulder may develop years later)
Adhesive capsulitis of the contralateral shoulder — develops in 15-30% of patients, typically within 5 years of the first shoulder; the second shoulder may be more severe in diabetic patients
Deltoid and rotator cuff deconditioning — from months of limited use; addressed through progressive strengthening exercises during the thawing phase
Prolonged disability affecting work and activities of daily living — the functional limitations (difficulty dressing, toileting, reaching overhead) significantly impact quality of life for 1-3 years
Progression of underlying medical conditions (diabetes, thyroid disease) — when frozen shoulder is the presenting sign of an undiagnosed metabolic condition
Iatrogenic complications from corticosteroid injection — rare but include facial flushing, temporary blood glucose elevation in diabetics, fat necrosis, and skin depigmentation

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Frozen Shoulder Patient Education
supportResources.description: AAOS patient education resources on frozen shoulder — symptoms, treatment options, and rehabilitation guidelines.

supportResources.name: American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Professional society for shoulder surgeons providing patient education on frozen shoulder and other shoulder conditions.

supportResources.name: NICE NG101: Shoulder Injury Guidelines. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2020.
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline addressing the assessment and management of shoulder pain including adhesive capsulitis.

supportResources.name: American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) — Frozen Shoulder Physical Therapy Resources
supportResources.description: APTA resources on physical therapy approaches to frozen shoulder, including stretching protocols.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: In a rotator cuff tear, passive range of motion is preserved (the examiner can lift the arm fully) but active motion is limited (the patient cannot lift it). In frozen shoulder, BOTH active and passive motion are restricted because the capsule itself is contracted. The drop arm test, external rotation lag sign, and muscle weakness testing distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impingement syndrome causes pain with overhead activities and a painful arc (pain between 60-120 degrees of abduction), but range of motion is preserved. Frozen shoulder causes global restriction of passive and active motion in all directions. They can coexist — impingement symptoms are common during the freezing phase of frozen shoulder.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acromioclavicular (AC) Joint Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AC joint arthritis causes localized pain at the top of the shoulder, worse with cross-body adduction (reaching across the body). Range of motion is generally preserved. X-ray shows AC joint space narrowing or osteophytes. Does not cause the global capsular restriction of frozen shoulder.

confusedWithConditions.name: Calcific Tendinitis of the Shoulder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Calcific tendinitis causes acute, severe shoulder pain with limited range of motion during an acute flare, but between flares the shoulder is typically mobile. X-ray shows calcific deposits in the rotator cuff. Frozen shoulder causes progressive, persistent capsular restriction regardless of flares.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glenohumeral Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Arthritis of the shoulder joint causes pain and stiffness, but the pain is typically activity-related and there is often crepitus (grinding) with motion. X-ray shows joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis. Can coexist with frozen shoulder as 'cuff tear arthropathy.'

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Frozen shoulder and rotator cuff tears frequently coexist — inflammatory cytokines from frozen shoulder can cause secondary rotator cuff irritation, and prolonged frozen shoulder can lead to rotator cuff disuse atrophy. The two must be distinguished because treatment differs: frozen shoulder requires stretching; rotator cuff tears may require rest and strengthening.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes is the strongest risk factor for frozen shoulder and influences its severity and treatment response. Diabetic patients with frozen shoulder should be referred to endocrinology for optimal glycemic management, which may improve frozen shoulder outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid disease is a significant risk factor for frozen shoulder. Any patient with bilateral frozen shoulder or recurrent frozen shoulder should have thyroid function screening. Treating the thyroid condition does not cure frozen shoulder but may prevent recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Impingement symptoms (painful arc, Neer/Hawkins sign) are common during the freezing phase of frozen shoulder and can be misleading. Distinguishing the primary pathology is important for treatment planning.

faqs.question: How long does frozen shoulder last?
faqs.answer: Frozen shoulder characteristically lasts 1-3 years even with optimal treatment — this is the natural history of the condition. The condition progresses through three phases: a painful freezing phase (2-9 months), a stiff frozen phase (4-12 months), and a gradual thawing phase (5-26 months). Treatment — physical therapy, corticosteroid injections, and in refractory cases manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic release — accelerates recovery but does not eliminate the underlying timeline. The condition eventually resolves in approximately 90% of patients, but patience over months to years is required.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to cure frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: There is no cure — only acceleration of the natural history. The fastest evidence-based approach combines: intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the glenohumeral joint (not the subacromial bursa) for pain control, consistent physical therapy 2-3 times per week with daily home exercises, heat before stretching, and NSAIDs for pain control. For patients who have not improved after 6-12 months of conservative treatment, manipulation under anesthesia or arthroscopic capsular release can provide more rapid dramatic improvement in range of motion — within days of the procedure rather than months.

faqs.question: What is the best exercise for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: The best exercise for frozen shoulder is a consistent, daily stretching program targeting all restricted ranges of motion. The most important stretches are: (1) Passive external rotation stretch — stand in a doorway with the elbow at 90 degrees and forearm pressed against the frame; rotate your body away to stretch the external rotators. Hold 30 seconds, repeat 5 times, twice daily. (2) Cross-body adduction stretch — pull the affected arm across your chest at shoulder height with the opposite hand. Hold 30 seconds, repeat 5 times, twice daily. (3) Forward flexion stretch — use the opposite arm to lift the affected arm overhead. Apply heat for 15-20 minutes before stretching to improve tissue extensibility.

faqs.question: Is frozen shoulder a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Frozen shoulder itself is not dangerous, but in some cases it can be a warning sign of an underlying systemic condition. The most important associations are: diabetes mellitus (5-fold increased risk — the frozen shoulder may be the first sign of undiagnosed diabetes), and thyroid disease (both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism are associated with increased risk). When frozen shoulder is bilateral, recurrent, or occurs in someone with other symptoms, screening blood tests (fasting glucose/HbA1c and thyroid function) are warranted. Beyond systemic conditions, frozen shoulder is a localized musculoskeletal condition that, while disabling, is not life-threatening.

faqs.question: Why is frozen shoulder more common in diabetics?
faqs.answer: People with diabetes have a 5-fold increased risk of developing frozen shoulder compared to non-diabetics. The mechanism involves glycosylation of collagen fibers in the joint capsule — excess glucose molecules attach to collagen fibers (forming advanced glycation end products or AGEs), making them stiffer, less extensible, and more prone to fibrotic contraction. This is the same process that causes diabetic complications in the eyes, kidneys, and nerves. Poorly controlled diabetes (higher HbA1c) is associated with more severe frozen shoulder. Diabetic patients also have more resistant frozen shoulder — it responds less well to treatment and is more likely to be bilateral.

faqs.question: Can I still use my arm with frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Yes — you should use your arm within pain limits. Complete immobilization worsens the condition because the capsule contracts further when not moved. However, you should avoid heavy lifting, forced overhead reaching, and activities that cause severe pain at the end range of motion. Within these limits, using the arm for light activities of daily living is beneficial — it maintains rotator cuff strength, prevents deconditioning, and the gentle movement within the available range may help preserve flexibility. Swimming and gentle water therapy are excellent ways to maintain activity while protecting the shoulder.

faqs.question: When is surgery needed for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Surgery for frozen shoulder is considered when 6-12 months of conservative treatment (physical therapy + corticosteroid injection) has not produced adequate improvement in range of motion and function. The two surgical options are: (1) Manipulation under anesthesia — forceful ranging of the shoulder while the patient is under general anesthesia to rupture the contracted capsule. (2) Arthroscopic capsular release — minimally invasive surgery cutting the contracted capsule. Both are followed by intensive physical therapy — without it, the capsule re-adheres and stiffness recurs. Surgery is not a first-line treatment and is reserved for refractory cases. Approximately 5-10% of frozen shoulder patients ultimately need a procedure.

faqs.question: Is frozen shoulder the same as arthritis?
faqs.answer: No — they are different conditions, though they can coexist. Arthritis (osteoarthritis) is degeneration of the joint cartilage, visible on X-ray as joint space narrowing, bone spurs, and硬化. Frozen shoulder is inflammation and fibrosis of the joint capsule, with normal X-rays in primary cases. Both cause pain and stiffness, but the mechanism differs. Some patients develop both conditions, called 'cuff tear arthropathy' when a rotator cuff tear leads to arthritis with associated capsular changes. X-rays distinguish them — arthritis shows bony changes; frozen shoulder typically shows a normal X-ray.

faqs.question: Can frozen shoulder come back after it heals?
faqs.answer: Yes — recurrence occurs in approximately 5-10% of patients in the same shoulder and 15-30% of patients develop frozen shoulder in the opposite (contralateral) shoulder. The second shoulder typically develops within 5 years of the first. Patients with diabetes have the highest recurrence rates. After recovery, maintaining a daily stretching program for both shoulders — particularly for external rotation and internal rotation — reduces the risk of recurrence. Any new shoulder stiffness should be evaluated promptly, as early treatment accelerates recovery.

faqs.question: What does frozen shoulder feel like?
faqs.answer: The primary sensation is stiffness — the shoulder feels tight and resistant to movement in all directions. In the freezing phase, this is accompanied by pain, often described as a deep, aching pain in the shoulder and upper arm, worse at night and at the end range of any movement. In the frozen phase, pain often decreases but stiffness becomes the dominant complaint — the arm simply will not move in certain directions no matter how hard you try. Patients commonly describe difficulty reaching behind the back (dressing, toileting), reaching overhead (shampooing hair, reaching cabinets), and rotating the arm outward (turning a steering wheel).

faqs.question: Is heat or ice better for frozen shoulder?
faqs.answer: Heat before stretching and ice after stretching — this is the optimal approach. Heat (warm compress, warm shower, heating pad for 15-20 minutes) increases blood flow, warms the collagen in the capsule, and makes it more extensible — allowing deeper, more effective stretching. Ice (ice pack for 15-20 minutes) after stretching reduces any inflammation triggered by the stretching and provides pain relief. During the acute painful freezing phase, ice alone may be more helpful for pain control; during the frozen phase when pain has decreased, heat before stretching is the primary modality.

metaTitle: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis): Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Frozen shoulder causes progressive shoulder pain and stiffness. Learn about stages, best exercises, corticosteroid injections, and surgery options. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Illustration showing the anatomy of the shoulder with the glenohumeral joint capsule highlighted
images.caption: Frozen shoulder involves inflammation and fibrosis of the glenohumeral joint capsule, causing progressive stiffness.

images.altText: Detailed diagram of the shoulder showing the glenohumeral joint capsule and rotator cuff
images.caption: The glenohumeral joint capsule normally allows approximately 150 degrees of flexion and 70 degrees of external rotation — both are significantly restricted in frozen shoulder.

images.altText: Illustration demonstrating frozen shoulder stretching exercises including external rotation stretch and cross-body adduction stretch
images.caption: Regular stretching exercises — twice daily — are the cornerstone of frozen shoulder treatment in all phases.
